title: Does CLIP’s generalization performance mainly stem from high train-test similarity?

reviews.summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.
reviews.strengths: The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.
reviews.weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.
reviews.questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.

Strengths:
The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.

Weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.

1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review for “Investigating the Role of Pretraining–Test Overlap versus Data Scale in CLIP’s Zero-Shot OOD Performance”

Summary  
This paper tackles an important question in vision–language pretraining: whether CLIP’s strong zero‐shot out‐of‐distribution (OOD) accuracy owes primarily to high perceptual overlap between its vast web‐scale pretraining data (LAION) and standard test benchmarks, or to the sheer scale and diversity of that data. To address this, the authors introduce two key concepts—perceptual similarity (measured by cosine similarity in CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding space) and the generalization gap (defined as the minimum embedding distance from each test image to any training image). They then conduct two pruning experiments on LAION: (1) iteratively removing images most similar to various ImageNet OOD sets to observe how zero‐shot performance degrades, and (2) pruning LAION until its similarity distribution matches that of ImageNet, then retraining CLIP and measuring accuracy. Strikingly, even after removing the high‐similarity overlap that ImageNet has, CLIP retains strong zero‐shot accuracy, suggesting that scale and diversity drive generalization more than train–test overlap.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and organization: The manuscript is very well written, with a logical flow that guides the reader through motivation, methodology, and results.  
2. Carefully designed experiments: Both pruning experiments are clearly defined and motivated, directly testing the competing hypotheses of overlap versus scale.  
3. Well‐motivated metrics: The introduction of perceptual similarity and the generalization gap provides a principled way to quantify overlap and its effect on OOD performance.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited scope of OOD benchmarks: All experiments are confined to ImageNet variants (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-A), which raises questions about the broader applicability of the findings.  
2. Lack of non–ImageNet datasets: There is no evaluation on other popular OOD or domain‐shift datasets such as iWildCam or FMoW, leaving uncertainty about whether the overlap‐versus‐scale conclusion holds in different domains.  
3. Model capacity considerations: The pruning and similarity measurements rely solely on embeddings from CLIP ViT-B/16; it remains unclear if the results generalize to larger or smaller CLIP architectures.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. To bolster claims of generality, include a second set of experiments on at least one non–ImageNet OOD benchmark (for example, iWildCam or FMoW). This would demonstrate whether the observed resilience to overlap pruning extends beyond ImageNet‐style data.  
2. Investigate whether using embeddings from larger CLIP models (e.g., ViT-L/14 or ViT-H/14) for computing perceptual similarity and pruning alters the pruning behavior or zero‐shot outcomes. This could reveal interactions between model capacity and data overlap.  
3. (Optional) Report a few qualitative examples of pruned versus retained LAION images to give intuition about what types of images are driving the generalization gap.

Overall Assessment  
This paper makes a valuable contribution to our understanding of why large‐scale contrastive pretraining yields strong zero‐shot robustness. The experimental design is sound and the results are compelling within the ImageNet setting. However, the current scope is somewhat narrow, and additional evaluation on non–ImageNet datasets would strengthen the generality of the conclusions.  

Recommendation: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold. I recommend acceptance, pending consideration of the above suggestions to broaden the empirical evaluation.

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer states that the paper investigates whether CLIP’s impressive zero-shot OOD performance stems from high similarity between its large pretraining set and test benchmarks or from sheer data scale. Through perceptual‐similarity pruning experiments on LAION vs. ImageNet, the authors find that even after matching train–test similarity to ImageNet, CLIP retains high accuracy, suggesting scale matters more than overlap.
strengths: Well–written and clearly structured manuscript
Experiments are clearly defined with explicit motivations
Introduction of perceptual similarity and generalization gap metrics is well motivated
weaknesses: All experiments focus on ImageNet variants, limiting generality
No evaluation on non–ImageNet OOD datasets (e.g., iWILDCam, FMoW)
Uncertainty whether conclusions hold beyond ImageNet‐style benchmarks

main_points: Paper examines if CLIP’s zero‐shot OOD performance is driven by train–test similarity or dataset scale
Defines perceptual similarity via pretrained CLIP ViT‐B/16 embeddings
Shows OOD sets like ImageNet‐Sketch have more matches in LAION than in ImageNet
Experiment 1: iteratively remove LAION images similar to the OOD set and retrain, observing performance drop
Experiment 2: prune LAION so its similarity gap matches ImageNet, then retrain CLIP—performance remains high

Generalization gap defined as set of minimum distances from each test image to training images
LAION pruning performed by removing samples above a similarity threshold or to match ImageNet’s similarity distribution
Zero‐shot top‐1 accuracy measured on standard ImageNet OOD benchmarks after each pruning stage

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.
reviews.questions: What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

Questions:
What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review for Paper “Investigating the Role of Train–Test Distribution Similarity in CLIP’s OOD Generalization”  
Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)

Summary  
This paper asks whether CLIP’s strong out-of-distribution (OOD) performance simply reflects the presence of train–test–similar images in its web-scale training set. To investigate this, the authors prune LAION‐400M by removing images whose CLIP embeddings are nearest neighbors to those in the test sets, thereby mimicking the train–test similarity gap observed in ImageNet-Train. Surprisingly, they find that even after discarding these highly similar examples, a CLIP model trained on a 100M-image subset of LAION achieves almost the same OOD accuracy as the full dataset. The results suggest that factors beyond mere image similarity—such as broader data diversity or scale—underpin CLIP’s generalization.

Strengths  
1. Novel perspective. Introducing the concept of train–test distribution similarity to analyze CLIP’s OOD behavior is insightful and addresses a gap in our understanding of large-scale contrastive models.  
2. Compelling empirical findings. Demonstrating that a 100M-image core subset can match the OOD performance of the full 400M-image corpus highlights the robustness of CLIP training and raises important questions about data efficiency.  
3. Well-motivated experiments. The pruning strategy—sequentially removing nearest-neighbor samples—directly tests the central hypothesis and is generally well conceived. The limited impact of removing top-similar images is a striking outcome.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited task scope. The evaluation is restricted to standard classification OOD benchmarks, whereas CLIP is widely used for retrieval and other multimodal tasks. This narrow focus may understate the role of train–test similarity in those settings.  
2. Incomplete methodological details. Key aspects of the nearest-neighbor filtering protocol are underspecified: How many neighbors are considered per test image? What CLIP similarity threshold defines a “near” sample? How exactly are subsets of 100M images sampled?  
3. Missing distributional comparison. The paper does not compare post-filtering similarity histograms of the pruned LAION subsets against those of ImageNet-Train. Without this, it remains unclear whether the pruned LAION still contains many borderline images that could bias OOD results.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Broaden evaluation to retrieval. For instance, filter LAION using in-domain captions from MS COCO, then test zero-shot retrieval on an out-of-domain set such as Flickr-30k to assess whether train–test similarity similarly affects retrieval performance.  
2. Add diverse benchmarks. Incorporate additional tasks like VTAB to probe whether the observed resilience of CLIP holds across different domains and challenge types.  
3. Fully specify filtering procedure. Report the number of neighbors used, the exact similarity cutoffs, and the selection algorithm for constructing each pruned split. This will ensure reproducibility and allow readers to gauge the strictness of the pruning.  
4. Compare similarity distributions. Plot and analyze histograms of CLIP similarity scores for both the filtered LAION splits and the ImageNet-Train set. Demonstrating that both have comparable similarity gaps would strengthen the core claim that CLIP’s OOD robustness is not merely due to seeing near-duplicate images.

Conclusion  
This work addresses an important question about the source of CLIP’s out-of-distribution generalization and delivers intriguing empirical evidence that challenges the notion that similarity alone drives performance. However, the paper would benefit from broader task evaluations and more transparent reporting of the pruning methodology. With these revisions, the contribution will be significantly strengthened. I recommend acceptance (Rating: 6), as the core idea is novel, the experiments are promising, and the findings will stimulate further research on data properties in large-scale contrastive learning.

Summary:
This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

Questions:
What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer finds that the paper investigates whether CLIP’s out-of-distribution generalization stems mainly from train–test distribution similarity by pruning LAION data via CLIP embeddings, and concludes that other factors beyond seen similar samples drive CLIP’s strong performance.
strengths: Introduces the novel perspective of train–test distribution similarity in analyzing CLIP’s OOD generalization.
Shows compelling results that a 100M-image subset of LAION-400M can still train a high-performing CLIP model on OOD benchmarks.
Experiments are well motivated and designed, demonstrating that removing nearest-neighbor samples has limited impact on performance.
weaknesses: Focuses only on common classification OOD benchmarks and ignores other important CLIP applications such as retrieval.
Crucial experimental details are missing, for example how the nearest-neighbor sets in Section 4.1 are constructed, how many samples are chosen, and what similarity threshold is used.

Does not compare the similarity distributions of the filtered LAION dataset and ImageNet-Train, leaving open the possibility that LAION retains many near-boundary samples and thus an unfair advantage.
suggestions: Evaluate CLIP on retrieval tasks by filtering with an in-domain dataset like MS COCO and testing on an out-of-domain set like Flickr-30k.
Include more diverse benchmarks such as VTAB to probe CLIP’s generalization.
Provide full details on nearest-neighbor construction: number of neighbors, CLIP score thresholds, and selection process.
Compare post-filtering similarity distributions between the pruned LAION splits and ImageNet-Train to validate that both share the same similarity gap.
main_points: Paper questions whether CLIP’s OOD performance is driven by high train–test similarity in web-scale data.
Uses CLIP embedding nearest-neighbor pruning to replicate ImageNet-Train’s similarity gap in LAION.
Finds that even after removing highly similar samples, CLIP retains strong OOD accuracy, implying other data properties matter.
Identifies a 100M-image core subset of LAION that matches full-scale performance on OOD benchmarks.
technical_details: Nearest-neighbor filtering is based on CLIP embedding similarity, but the criteria (number of neighbors, similarity cutoff) are not specified.
Construction of LAION subsets (e.g., size and sampling strategy) lacks explicit description.
No clear description of how many samples are pruned or retained at each similarity threshold.
Missing comparison of similarity histograms or distributions between filtered LAION and ImageNet-Train.

reviews.summary: The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.
reviews.strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"
reviews.questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper explores whether CLIP’s strong out-of-distribution (OOD) performance is merely a by‐product of high train–test image similarity. By pruning LAION-400M to remove images highly similar to each OOD test sample (e.g., sketches, cartoons), the authors demonstrate a marked drop in CLIP’s zero‐shot accuracy. Crucially, when they adjust the pruning extent to match the train–test similarity gap of ImageNet‐trained models, CLIP continues to outperform those baselines, suggesting that its OOD robustness cannot be explained solely by overlapping training content.

Strengths  
1. Key empirical finding: Even after substantially reducing train–test similarity, CLIP retains a significant performance edge over ImageNet-pretrained models.  
2. Rigorous, large‐scale experiments: The computational effort to prune millions of images and evaluate multiple OOD benchmarks is commendable and provides valuable data points for the community.  
3. Open science: The authors release both the pruned datasets and code, which will help others reproduce and extend this line of inquiry.

Weaknesses  
1. Incomplete OOD exclusion: The pruning strategy removes only those images within a nearest-perceptual-neighbor threshold of each test sample. Entire domains (e.g., all sketches) remain partly represented by examples beyond that threshold.  
2. Residual domain leakage: Classes analogous to the test set—such as “space shuttle” sketches not explicitly in the sketch benchmark—may still be present in training, weakening the claim of a fair OOD setting.  
3. Terminology clash: The use of “generalization gap” to describe the train–test similarity difference overlaps with established notions of generalization gap in learning theory, potentially causing confusion.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Enforce true OOD removal. Rather than threshold-based pruning, consider excluding all images from a given test domain (for instance, every sketch‐style example) to ensure no domain‐specific content remains in training.  
2. Clarify and streamline the abstract and contributions. A more concise framing of the problem and key findings would improve readability.  
3. Rename the “generalization gap.” Adopting a term like “similarity gap” or “train–test overlap metric” would avoid conflicts with standard terminology.  
4. Additional experiments. To solidify the claims, repeat the core evaluations after fully removing each test domain (e.g., all sketch images) from the training corpus.

Detailed Commentary  
• Pruning Methodology: The nearest-perceptual-neighbor approach (Abstract Figure 1) is insightful, but its reliance on a threshold means many domain‐relevant samples slip through.  
• Sketch Domain Coverage: As noted, the benchmark’s “sketch” category omits some object classes—yet visually similar sketches of those objects may still appear in LAION-400M, granting CLIP indirect exposure.  
• Performance Analyses: The matched‐gap experiments effectively isolate the contribution of similarity, but a stricter domain exclusion would strengthen the argument that CLIP’s architecture and training scheme confer inherent OOD advantages.

Decision: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)  
While the paper makes a compelling case that CLIP’s OOD gains are not purely due to overlapping content, the current pruning protocol does not fully enforce a domain-level OOD split. Addressing the suggestions above would greatly enhance its rigor. Nonetheless, given the importance of the question, the scale of the experiments, and the promising initial results, I recommend marginal acceptance.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.
reviews.strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?
reviews.questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.

Strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?

Questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper investigates whether CLIP’s impressive zero-shot and out-of-distribution (OOD) performance can be attributed in large part to a high degree of train–test similarity in its web-scale LAION-400M training set. By first demonstrating that LAION contains images semantically and stylistically similar to common OOD benchmarks (Fig. 1), the authors quantify the relationship between a test sample’s nearest-neighbor similarity (using cosine similarity in the CLIP ViT-B/16 image-embedding space) and zero-shot accuracy (Fig. 3). They then introduce similarity-based pruning strategies—near-pruning (removing the highest-similarity samples), far-pruning (removing the lowest-similarity samples), and random pruning—and show in Fig. 4 that near-pruning LAION for samples similar to benchmarks like ImageNet-Sketch leads to substantially larger accuracy drops than random pruning. They further attempt corrective pruning to align LAION’s similarity distribution to that of ImageNet-train and observe that CLIP retains much of its performance, suggesting additional factors contribute to its generalization.

Strengths  
1. The paper compellingly illustrates that LAION-400M contains many images highly similar to OOD benchmarks, a phenomenon not observed in the standard ImageNet training set (Fig. 1).  
2. The quantitative analysis in Fig. 3 clearly demonstrates a strong correlation between a test image’s nearest-neighbor similarity in LAION and CLIP’s zero-shot accuracy, with OOD samples having on average higher similarity scores than ImageNet-Val (excluding the validation set itself).  
3. The pruning experiments (Fig. 4) provide evidence that removing samples most similar to ImageNet-Sketch or ImageNet-Val causes a larger degradation in CLIP performance compared to random pruning, underlining the practical impact of these overlapping examples on robustness.

Weaknesses  
1. It remains unclear whether pruning samples highly similar to one benchmark (e.g., Sketch) specifically degrades performance only on that task or whether it induces a global drop across all benchmarks. The specificity of pruning effects is not sufficiently characterized.  
2. The study does not include a direct comparison with an ImageNet-trained model (e.g., ResNet) subjected to the same pruning regime. Such a comparison is essential to determine if CLIP’s OOD robustness truly depends more heavily on high-similarity examples.  
3. The manuscript lacks a clear, overarching discussion of what “zero-shot learning” and “out-of-distribution robustness” entail in this context, leaving readers uncertain about the broader implications of the findings.  
4. Sections 5 and 6 propose “corrective pruning” to worsen performance by design, but the motivation and practical utility of deliberately removing helpful examples remain ambiguous.  
5. Because the pruning leverages information about test-distribution similarity, potential transductive learning or licensing issues arise. The paper does not address whether exposing test-set characteristics in training selection violates standard evaluation protocols or data licenses.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Assess pruning specificity by measuring CLIP’s accuracy on both ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch after performing near-pruning based solely on Sketch similarity. This would clarify whether the effect is benchmark-specific.  
2. Apply the same near-, far-, and random-pruning protocol to a standard ImageNet-trained model (e.g., ResNet50), ideally pruning by the same percentage of high-similarity samples, and compare the resulting performance drops to CLIP’s.  
3. Enrich the conceptual framing with clear definitions of zero-shot learning and OOD robustness, and discuss what practitioners should conclude about deploying CLIP in settings without exact train–test overlap.  
4. Clarify the rationale behind Sections 5/6: if practitioners would never intentionally prune out high-similarity samples, what real-world scenarios or insights justify corrective pruning?  
5. Explicitly address any transductive learning concerns or licensing constraints that arise when test-distribution similarity guides training-set selection.

Overall Assessment  
This work sheds light on an important factor—train–test similarity—in CLIP’s out-of-distribution performance and introduces a novel pruning-based analysis. The empirical results are intriguing and well supported by figures. However, key questions about the specificity of pruning effects, appropriate baselines (ImageNet-trained models), and the broader conceptual and practical framing remain open. I find the paper interesting but marginally below the bar for acceptance in its current form (Rating: 5). Further experiments and clarification would strengthen its contribution to our understanding of zero-shot and OOD robustness.

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer summarizes that the paper tests whether CLIP’s out-of-distribution performance arises mainly from high train-test similarity by pruning LAION images most similar to OOD benchmarks and measuring accuracy drops upon retraining.
strengths: Figure 1 demonstrates that LAION-400M contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks, unlike ImageNet-train.

Figure 3 quantitatively shows that OOD samples’ nearest neighbors in LAION-400M are on average more similar than those in ImageNet-train (except on ImageNet-Val) and that CLIP accuracy correlates with neighbor similarity.

Figure 4 reveals that pruning LAION for samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val causes a substantially larger accuracy drop than random pruning, highlighting the importance of those examples for CLIP’s robustness.
weaknesses: The pruning experiments (Fig. 4) are inconclusive regarding specificity: it remains unclear whether near-pruned samples only affect one benchmark or degrade performance across all tasks.
No comparison is made to ImageNet-trained models under the same pruning regime to see if CLIP loses OOD performance faster or differently.
The paper lacks a broader conceptual discussion of “zero-shot” and “out-of-distribution robustness,” leaving readers unclear about definitions and implications.
Sections 5 and 6 aim to “correct for highly similar images,” but it is unclear why one would deliberately prune to worsen performance or what practical guidance follows.
Potential transductive learning or licensing issues arise because the pruning method effectively exposes test distributions during training.
suggestions: Evaluate pruning specificity by measuring accuracy on both ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch after pruning samples similar only to Sketch.
Apply the same pruning protocol to an ImageNet-trained model (e.g., ResNet) and compare its performance drop to that of CLIP.
Include a broader discussion defining zero-shot learning and OOD robustness, and clarify what conclusions practitioners should draw about CLIP’s capabilities.
Explain the motivation and practical use cases for Sections 5/6: why prune training data to decrease benchmark performance if those samples never exactly appear in training?
Address possible transductive learning concerns and data-license constraints when test distributions inform training data selection.
main_points: The paper hypothesizes that CLIP’s OOD success may be driven by high train-test similarity in web-scale datasets like LAION.
They show a strong correlation between a test sample’s nearest-neighbor similarity in LAION-400M and CLIP’s zero-shot accuracy.
Similarity-based pruning (near-pruning) causes larger drops in CLIP performance than random or far-pruning.
LAION-400M contains more samples similar to OOD benchmarks than ImageNet-train, suggesting data overlap influences measured robustness.
After pruning highly similar images, CLIP still retains much of its performance, implying other factors drive generalization.
The paper formalizes a similarity gap and conducts corrective pruning to align LAION’s similarity distribution with ImageNet-train.
technical_details: Similarity metric: cosine similarity in CLIP ViT-B/16+ image embedding space to identify nearest neighbors.
Pruning strategies: near-pruning (drop highest-similarity samples), far-pruning (drop lowest), and rand-pruning at varying dataset fractions.
Recommendation to prune ImageNet-train by X% to compare performance decay rates with CLIP.
Consideration of transductive learning risks if test-set similarity guides training-set pruning.
Reference to specific figures (Figs. 1, 3, 4, 7) to illustrate neighbor distributions and accuracy correlations.

title: Simple Hierarchical Planning with Diffusion

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.
reviews.strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.
reviews.weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.
reviews.questions: See weakness above.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.

Strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.

Weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.

Questions:
See weakness above.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review for “Hierarchical Diffuser (HD): A Two-Stage Diffusion Model for Scalable Planning”

Summary  
This paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a two-level extension of the Diffuser framework that first generates sparse sub-goals via a high-level diffusion model and then fills in low-level actions for each segment in parallel. Empirically, HD achieves a larger effective receptive field, better out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization, and substantially reduced planning time compared to the original Diffuser. The authors show that even a sparse-only version (SD) benefits from increased receptive field and that adding dense actions at the high level (SD-DA) further improves return prediction. However, the core idea—stacking two existing Diffuser modules—and several implementation and architectural details leave important questions open.

Strengths  
1. Decomposing long-horizon planning into sub-goals and segment-level diffusion makes the low-level planner task-agnostic and more scalable to diverse scenarios.  
2. The sparse-only diffuser (SD) outperforms the vanilla Diffuser mainly by expanding the receptive field, as confirmed by kernel-size ablations.  
3. Augmenting the sparse module with dense actions (SD-DA) addresses a common hierarchical-RL bottleneck by enhancing the return-prediction guidance signal.  
4. Parallelizing the planning of low-level segments, combined with a shorter high-level sequence, yields significant computation savings, roughly halving planning time relative to Diffuser.

Weaknesses  
1. Novelty is limited: HD essentially layers two off-the-shelf Diffuser modules (sparse and standard) without introducing a fundamentally new architecture.  
2. The paper lacks ablations against state-only diffusion baselines (e.g., Decision-Diffuser) to justify the added complexity of including actions in the high-level guidance.  
3. It remains unclear whether HD uses diffusion-sampled actions or a separate controller (e.g., PD-controller as in Diffuser) on Maze2D tasks. This detail affects reproducibility and performance interpretation.  
4. Independent planning of sub-goal segments may lead to non-optimal or overlapping trajectories, yet no mechanism is described to enforce segment consistency or stitching.  
5. There is no feedback loop from the low-level diffuser back to the high-level planner, raising concerns about sub-goal execution errors and overall robustness.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Include an ablation comparing HD against a state-only diffusion baseline such as Decision-Diffuser, to validate the necessity of action-conditioned guidance for return estimation.  
2. Clearly state how actions are realized on Maze2D tasks—whether they are sampled via diffusion or generated by an external controller—and report any performance differences.  
3. Describe how the method prevents or resolves overlapping and non-optimal segments when high-level sub-goals are planned independently.  
4. Specify the number of diffusion samples drawn for each sub-goal sequence and for each low-level segment, as this sampling budget directly influences planning quality and computational cost.  
5. Elaborate on any mechanisms (e.g., inverse dynamics, replanning strategies) that handle failures to reach exact sub-goals and provide feedback to the high-level planner.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, Hierarchical Diffuser is a promising step toward scaling diffusion-based planning through hierarchical decomposition. The demonstrated gains in receptive field, return prediction, OOD generalization, and runtime efficiency are noteworthy. Nonetheless, the paper would benefit from stronger empirical justification of its design choices and more thorough discussion of implementation details and failure modes. I recommend a marginally above-threshold acceptance (Rating: 6) given the solid empirical contributions and the potential for broader impact once the outlined concerns are addressed.

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer finds the proposed Hierarchical Diffuser (HD) to be a promising extension of Diffuser, demonstrating improved receptive field, OOD generalization, and computation efficiency, but feels the core formulation lacks novelty and raises questions about certain design choices.
strengths: Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models enables solving more diverse scenarios and makes low-level diffusion planning task-agnostic.
Even the sparse diffuser (SD) outperforms the original Diffuser thanks to an increased receptive field.
Including dense actions in the sparse diffuser improves return-prediction, addressing a common hierarchical-RL bottleneck.
HD reduces planning time by using a shorter high-level sequence and parallel low-level segment planning, resulting in lower computation overhead than Diffuser.
weaknesses: Limited novelty: HD essentially stacks two existing Diffuser modules (one sparse, one standard) without fundamentally new architecture.
No ablation comparing to state-only diffusion methods (e.g., Decision-Diffuser) to justify why including actions in guidance is necessary.
Unclear whether the implementation uses diffusion-sampled actions or a separate controller on Maze2D tasks, as in Diffuser.
Independent sub-goal planning may produce non-optimal or overlapping segments, with no mechanism ensuring consistency between segments.
Lack of feedback from the low-level diffuser back to the high-level module raises concerns about reaching exact sub-goals and robustness to execution errors.
suggestions: Include ablation studies comparing HD to state-only diffusion (Decision-Diffuser) to validate the need for action diffusion in return estimation.
Clarify implementation details: whether diffusion-sampled actions or an external controller is used for Maze2D tasks.
Explain how the method prevents segment overlap or non-optimal stitching when sub-goals are planned independently.
Specify how many trajectories are sampled for each sub-goal sequence and for each segment during planning.
Describe the mechanism (if any) for handling cases where low-level actions fail to reach the exact planned sub-goal and how to provide feedback to the high-level planner.
main_points: HD builds on Diffuser by introducing a sparse high-level diffuser for sub-goal planning and a low-level diffuser for segment completion.
Sparse Diffuser benefits largely from a larger receptive field, which improves performance at the cost of generalization as seen by kernel-size ablations.
Including dense actions at the high level (SD-DA) aids the guidance function in predicting returns more accurately.
Hierarchical planning halves the effective trajectory length at the high level and parallelizes low-level segments, yielding significant time savings.
Concerns exist about architectural novelty, implementation clarity, and consistency between independently planned sub-goal segments.
technical_details: Decision-Diffuser performs state-only diffusion and could serve as a baseline for action-free guidance ablations.
Maze2D tasks may require either diffusion-sampled actions or a PD-controller as in the original Diffuser implementation.
Kernel-size ablations showed that increasing receptive field improves in-distribution performance but worsens OOD generalization.
Sub-goal overlap and execution error are not addressed by the current two-stage planning; no inverse dynamics or replanning feedback is described.
Number of samples per sub-goal sequence and per segment during guidance sampling is unspecified and affects planning robustness.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes an hierarchical variant of Diffuser. The algorithm involves using a high-level planner that plans trajectories in a sparser manner and a low-level planner that adds detailed states to the trajectories produces by the high-level planner. Experiment result demonstrate that hierarchical planning produces better performance in long-horizon planning tasks and offline RL tasks. The authers also study the impact of kernel size in Diffuser and the impact of the jumpy steps. An interesting observation is that Diffuser with a small receptive field can not cover the data distribution well.
reviews.strengths:
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The method is straightforward and demonstrates strong results in long-horizon planning tasks, especially the AntMaze.
3. The study of relationship between receptive field of the diffusion model and the data coverage ratio is interesting and offers valuable insight for futher research on diffusion models for planning in more complex domains.
reviews.weaknesses:
One minor weakness is that the baseline Decision Diffuser[1] is missing. Also the study presented in this paper focuses on using the CNN architecture as Diffuser does. A comparison between using CNN and transformer architecutre, such as Decision Diffuser, would be encouraged.

[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.
reviews.questions:
1. According to Fig 3., Diffuser with a small kernel size covers one mode of the data distribution during actual execution. Is it the case that the standard Diffuser is able to \textbf{cover} the full data distribution but just fails to achieve full data coverage during actual execution? If so, what might be the cause the such gap?
2. Still concerning Fig 3., although HD seems to cover most of the data distribution, HD fails to cover two short segments/branches. Why does such pheomena occur?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes an hierarchical variant of Diffuser. The algorithm involves using a high-level planner that plans trajectories in a sparser manner and a low-level planner that adds detailed states to the trajectories produces by the high-level planner. Experiment result demonstrate that hierarchical planning produces better performance in long-horizon planning tasks and offline RL tasks. The authers also study the impact of kernel size in Diffuser and the impact of the jumpy steps. An interesting observation is that Diffuser with a small receptive field can not cover the data distribution well.

Strengths:
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The method is straightforward and demonstrates strong results in long-horizon planning tasks, especially the AntMaze.
3. The study of relationship between receptive field of the diffusion model and the data coverage ratio is interesting and offers valuable insight for futher research on diffusion models for planning in more complex domains.

Weaknesses:
One minor weakness is that the baseline Decision Diffuser[1] is missing. Also the study presented in this paper focuses on using the CNN architecture as Diffuser does. A comparison between using CNN and transformer architecutre, such as Decision Diffuser, would be encouraged.

[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.

Questions:
1. According to Fig 3., Diffuser with a small kernel size covers one mode of the data distribution during actual execution. Is it the case that the standard Diffuser is able to \textbf{cover} the full data distribution but just fails to achieve full data coverage during actual execution? If so, what might be the cause the such gap?
2. Still concerning Fig 3., although HD seems to cover most of the data distribution, HD fails to cover two short segments/branches. Why does such pheomena occur?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review for “Hierarchical Diffusion-Based Planning with Sparse High-Level Modules”

Summary  
This paper introduces a hierarchical variant of Diffuser, combining a sparse high-level planning module with a dense low-level refinement module. The key idea is to decompose long-horizon trajectory synthesis into coarse, “jumpy” steps (K) at the high level, followed by fine-grained diffusion at the lower level, thereby improving coverage of the data distribution over long horizons. Empirical results on standard benchmarks (e.g., AntMaze) demonstrate that the hierarchical approach outperforms a flat Diffuser in both long-horizon planning and offline RL tasks. The authors further present an insightful analysis of how the diffusion model’s receptive field (kernel size) impacts its ability to cover multiple modes in the data, showing that small kernels lead to mode collapse and that even the hierarchical model can miss short segments under certain settings.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and Presentation: The manuscript is well written and easy to follow. The hierarchical architecture is motivated clearly, and the overall structure of the paper guides the reader smoothly from motivation to implementation and evaluation.  
2. Empirical Performance: The proposed method achieves strong results on challenging long-horizon tasks, notably outperforming the flat Diffuser baseline on AntMaze benchmarks. Quantitative improvements are substantial and consistent across multiple metrics.  
3. Analytical Insight: The study of kernel size versus data coverage is particularly valuable. Figure 3 effectively illustrates that a small receptive field causes traditional Diffuser to cover only a single mode, and the hierarchical model—while better—still misses some short trajectory branches. This analysis deepens our understanding of diffusion-based planners.

Weaknesses  
1. Missing Baseline Comparison: The paper does not include Decision Diffuser (Ajay et al., ICLR 2023) as a baseline, which is an important recent diffusion-based planning method.  
2. Architecture Scope: All experiments are conducted with a CNN‐based diffusion backbone. It remains unclear how the hierarchical approach generalizes to transformer‐based architectures, which have become standard in diffusion planning.

Suggestions for Improvement  
– Incorporate Decision Diffuser into the empirical comparisons to position the hierarchical method relative to the current state of the art.  
– Evaluate the hierarchical planning framework using a transformer‐based diffusion model (e.g., Decision Diffuser) to assess whether the observed gains persist across different backbone architectures.  

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this work presents a straightforward yet impactful extension to Diffuser, yielding significant gains in long-horizon planning and offering valuable insights into diffusion model coverage. Despite the missing comparison to Decision Diffuser and the limited architectural scope, the paper makes a solid contribution to the field. I recommend acceptance with minor revisions.  

Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)
